As these flying machines became more common, it was wise for people to learn how to recognize the different types that might be passing overhead. Most would be the smaller, commercial drones—used for delivering Amazon packages, transporting medical supplies, conducting real-estate surveys, and even carrying mail. But for those living near the country’s borders, the view from above would be far more intense. These areas would see a steady presence of heavy-duty military drones, like the MQ-9 Reaper, built by General Atomics.  Yet, you didn’t always have to see a drone to know it was near. In Virginia, an inventor named Brian Hearing, Ph.D., had developed a new kind of drone-detection technology. It didn’t rely on sight, but on sound—specifically, the unique noise signature of an approaching drone. The idea had come to him after a strange and unexpected event. His business partner had lost control of a drone, which had flown into their neighbor’s backyard. As they watched from a distance, they were stunned by the clarity of the footage the drone captured. Inside the neighbor’s home, the family sat completely unaware, their every move recorded in high definition. It was this moment that sparked Hearing’s determination to find a way to detect these silent, watchful machines before they got too close.

Saudi officials reportedly considered announcing that journalist Jamal Khashoggi died accidentally inside the Saudi consulate in Istanbul, where he had gone to obtain documents for a marriage license on October 2. The idea came after several news outlets reported late Monday that an interrogation had gone awry. According to The New York Times, Saudi Arabia had planned to release a report claiming Khashoggi’s death occurred two weeks ago.  A source told CNN that the report is expected to admit the interrogation occurred without proper authorization and that Saudi Arabia would take action against those responsible. Khashoggi, a Washington Post columnist, has not been seen since entering the consulate on October 2. Evidence suggests he was killed and dismembered inside the building.  Turkish officials reportedly revealed last week that they have audio and video evidence showing Khashoggi was interrogated, tortured, and then murdered. The New Yorker’s Robin Wright wrote last week that Khashoggi had feared for his life, saying, “Of course they’d like to see me out of the picture,” according to a conversation she had with him.  President Donald Trump commented Monday that “rogue killers” may be responsible for Khashoggi’s disappearance, adding that Saudi Arabia’s King Salman “firmly denied” any knowledge of the incident. While expressing concern over Khashoggi’s fate, Trump said he would not halt arms sales to the country.  Secretary of State Mike Pompeo made an urgent trip to Saudi Arabia on Monday to meet with King Salman.

Experts suggest a 500-calorie deficit per day to get the process going.  **Character B:** But can’t I just work out a lot to burn 500 calories?  **Character A:** You can, but it’s not sustainable. High-intensity workouts that burn that much are tough to keep up with long-term.  **Character B:** So what’s the better approach?  **Character A:** Cutting out high-calorie foods is easier and more effective. But you can’t rely on diet alone either.  **Character B:** Why not?  **Character A:** If you cut too much, you risk food deprivation and missing out on nutrients. You need balance.  **Character B:** I see. So you’re saying diet is the main driver, but you still need exercise?  **Character A:** Exactly. “Abs are made in the kitchen,” but they become visible with the right workouts.  **Character B:** So if I want to cut 600 calories a day, I should split it?  **Character A:** Yes—cut 300 through diet and burn 300 through exercise. It’s more balanced and sustainable.  **Character B:** And what about the benefits of working out?  **Character A:** It boosts metabolism, builds muscle, and tones your body—things diet alone won’t do.  **Character B:** So the key is combining both?  **Character A:** Definitely. A healthy diet and regular exercise lead to sustainable weight loss and a better physique.  **Character B:** I’ve heard the 80/20 rule before, but it makes more sense now.  **Character A:** That’s the takeaway—diet is more important, but exercise is still essential.

evidence_spans:
**Character A:** Have you ever heard that weight loss is mostly about diet?

**Character A:** Actually, it’s the 80-20 rule—80% diet and 20% exercise. Without a calorie deficit, you won’t lose weight.

**Character B:** So you’re saying if I eat less than I burn, I’ll lose weight?

**Character A:** Exactly. Experts suggest a 500-calorie deficit per day to get the process going.

**Character A:** You can, but it’s not sustainable. High-intensity workouts that burn that much are tough to keep up with long-term.

**Character A:** Cutting out high-calorie foods is easier and more effective. But you can’t rely on diet alone either.

**Character B:** Why not?

**Character B:** And what about the benefits of working out?

**Character A:** It boosts metabolism, builds muscle, and tones your body—things diet alone won’t do.

**Character B:** So the key is combining both?

**Character A:** Definitely. A healthy diet and regular exercise lead to sustainable weight loss and a better physique.

**Character B:** I’ve heard the 80/20 rule before, but it makes more sense now.

**Character A:** That’s the takeaway—diet is more important, but exercise is still essential.

**Team Meeting Dialogue:**

**Sarah:** Okay, so I just read this article about Judge David Miller changing his mind on the bond status for Adam Kaufman. You know, the Aventura guy accused of killing his wife.

**James:** Yeah, I saw that too. The article says he had an epiphany on Father’s Day and reconvened court this week to reconsider releasing Kaufman, even though he had previously denied it.

**Lena:** That’s actually pretty admirable, right? For a judge to re-evaluate a tough decision like that. It shows he’s not just sticking to his first ruling for the sake of it.

**James:** Definitely. It’s not easy to admit you might have made the wrong call, especially in a high-profile case. I think it shows real integrity.

[Your Name]   [Your Address]   [City, State, ZIP Code]   [Email Address]   [Date]    To: Mr. Pete [Last Name]        [Publication Name]        [Address]        [City, State, ZIP Code]    Dear Mr. [Last Name],    I hope this letter finds you well. I am writing to express my appreciation for your recent article, as well as to share a personal experience that underscores the growing importance of high-definition technology in both entertainment and professional settings.    Last summer, while on a business trip to Atlanta, I attended a game between the Braves and the Boston Red Sox at Turner Field. My goal was to capture some photos of the team, particularly David Ortiz and Manny Ramirez. Unfortunately, my point-and-shoot Canon camera lacked the necessary zoom to do so. It was then that I realized I could capture clearer images by focusing on the Mitsubishi high-definition screen in centerfield—something that also appears on your publication’s cover. Not only did this provide a better vantage point, but it also allowed me to document the entire Red Sox lineup as they came to bat.    This experience made me reflect on the transformative power of HD. High-definition is more than a visual enhancement—it is a shift in how we perceive and expect to experience content. As your article "One of These Things is Not Like the Others" suggests, clients are increasingly bringing their HD expectations from home into the workplace. They now demand similar quality in professional environments, often at consumer-level prices. While this can be a challenge for integrators, some are rising to meet the demand.    At last month’s InfoComm, the topic of HD over the network—especially in video teleconferencing—was a focal point. Crestron president George Feldstein emphasized the need for dedicated AV networks to ensure sufficient bandwidth, a view shared by many in the industry. Similarly, Rick Snyder of Tandberg Americas noted that while most clients purchase systems with HD capabilities, only a minority actually utilize them.    Clearly, HD is on the path to becoming a de facto standard. In the long term, future-proofing systems will be essential. However, in the short term, aligning client expectations with technological realities will remain a continuous challenge.    We couldn’t agree more with your insights, Mr. [Last Name]. On behalf of myself and the readers of PRO AV, we are truly fortunate to have you contributing to our publication. Thank you for your thought-provoking work.    Warm regards,    [Your Full Name]   [Your Title]   [Your Company Name]

SOURCE=doc_1 CHUNK=1 SCORE=1.0000
A man was arrested on Monday at Kuala Lumpur International Airport in Malaysia for attempting to smuggle endangered snakes. According to reports, the zipper on the suspect’s luggage malfunctioned, causing the bag to open and revealing a large number of reptiles. The haul included 95 endangered boa constrictors, as well as several non-endangered snakes and turtles. The arrest followed the discovery of the boas, as it is illegal to export endangered species without the appropriate permits.  The suspect, identified as Anson Wong, is not a first-time offender. National Geographic reports that Wong is widely regarded as a major figure in the global wildlife smuggling trade. Bryan Christy of National Geographic notes that Wong, based in Penang, runs one of the world’s largest legal reptile supply companies, which has previously been used as a cover for smuggling critically endangered wildlife from countries including Australia, China, Madagascar, New Zealand, and South America. His illicit trade has reportedly included items such as snow leopard pelts, panda bear skins, rhino horns, rare birds, and Komodo dragons.  Wong was previously arrested in 1990 as part of a five-year sting operation conducted by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service. He served prison time until his release in 2003. His latest arrest has resulted in a six-month prison sentence and a fine of $61,000. Authorities also seized Wong’s laptop and cell phone, which may provide valuable information to help dismantle other smuggling operations.  Wildlife smuggling remains a pervasive issue due to the relatively low risk of detection and prosecution. Christy explains that wildlife trafficking is among the most profitable forms of transnational organized crime, not only due to high profit margins but also because of the minimal likelihood of being caught. Law enforcement agencies worldwide are often underfunded and understaffed in their efforts to combat the trade. Even when smugglers are apprehended, penalties are typically limited to fines that are often as minor as a parking ticket.  Christy views Wong’s recent arrest as a positive development, suggesting that law enforcement agencies are beginning to make progress in addressing the global wildlife smuggling trade.

Use the evidence above when it is relevant. If it is not relevant, answer from the prompt.

**Interviewer:** Thanks for joining us today. For those who might be new to SkillShow, can you explain what kind of information people can find on your FAQ page?  **Expert:** Absolutely. Whether you're new to SkillShow or a long-time user, our FAQ page is a great resource for the most common questions. If you can’t find what you’re looking for there, we encourage you to send us an email and someone will be in touch quickly.  **Interviewer:** What can people expect in a SkillShow Premium Video?  **Expert:** Premium Videos typically include a player introduction, multiple camera angles of your primary defensive skills, multiple angles of batting practice, and your 60-yard dash. It’s designed to give a comprehensive look at a player’s abilities.  **Interviewer:** How do customers receive their videos?  **Expert:** After the video is edited, you’ll receive a digital copy via email that you can download for your own use. For Perfect Game players, the video will also be posted on their PG Profile Page for easy access and sharing.  **Interviewer:** How long does the editing process usually take?  **Expert:** Our standard editing time is about four weeks, but we also offer a one-week rush edit option for those who need it sooner.  **Interviewer:** Can you film a player during an actual game?  **Expert:** No, we don’t recommend filming during games for a quality skills video. There are too many variables, and it’s difficult to capture consistent performance. Coaches prefer a ‘pro-style’ workout for evaluation. That said, we do film pitchers during games, but only in between innings.  **Interviewer:** What if a player isn’t happy with their video?  **Expert:** We want to make sure you’re completely satisfied. You’ll have up to one week to review your video and request any changes if needed.  **Interviewer:** Why is having a video so important for a player’s recruitment?  **Expert:** A coach needs to see your skills to determine if you can play at their level. A resume alone doesn’t allow them to evaluate your projectability as an athlete. Your video is your resume—it’s your first and often only chance to make a strong impression. It’s the primary way many players get in front of the schools they’re interested in.  **Interviewer:** When is the best time to start creating a video?  **Expert:** You can never start too early. College coaches are always looking ahead. The earlier you get on their radar, the better. In the past, recruiting often began in a player’s senior year, but now verbal commitments are being offered to sophomores and even freshmen.  **Interviewer:** How often should a player update their video?  **Expert:** At least once a year, or any time you feel like your game has changed. That could be when you’ve gotten bigger, stronger, faster, or when you’ve improved your mechanics since your last video.  **Interviewer:** Is there a specific time of year when coaches are less interested in recruiting?  **Expert:** That’s a common misconception. Coaches are in recruiting mode all year long. College teams are constantly turning over players due to graduation, injury, the draft, or lack of playing time. A coach needs a database of potential recruits to keep their team strong each year. A video is a great tool to get on a coach’s radar and make it easy for them to follow your progress. In fact, most coaches prefer to see a video when a player reaches out.

Use the evidence above when it is relevant. If it is not relevant, answer from the prompt.

**Expert:** We want to make sure you’re completely satisfied. You’ll have up to one week to review your video and request any changes if needed.

**Interviewer:** Why is having a video so important for a player’s recruitment?

**Expert:** A coach needs to see your skills to determine if you can play at their level. A resume alone doesn’t allow them to evaluate your projectability as an athlete. Your video is your resume—it’s your first and often only chance to make a strong impression. It’s the primary way many players get in front of the schools they’re interested in.

**Interviewer:** When is the best time to start creating a video?

**Expert:** You can never start too early. College coaches are always looking ahead. The earlier you get on their radar, the better. In the past, recruiting often began in a player’s senior year, but now verbal commitments are being offered to sophomores and even freshmen.

**Interviewer:** How often should a player update their video?

**Expert:** At least once a year, or any time you feel like your game has changed. That could be when you’ve gotten bigger, stronger, faster, or when you’ve improved your mechanics since your last video.

**Interviewer:** Is there a specific time of year when coaches are less interested in recruiting?

**Expert:** That’s a common misconception. Coaches are in recruiting mode all year long. College teams are constantly turning over players due to graduation, injury, the draft, or lack of playing time. A coach needs a database of potential recruits to keep their team strong each year. A video is a great tool to get on a coach’s radar and make it easy for them to follow your progress. In fact, most coaches prefer to see a video when a player reaches out.

I knew I was going to write a post the moment I saw the meme. Something about it struck a chord, like it was a sign or a nudge from the universe. The meme was about the ongoing construction on I-85 in South Carolina—years of it, stretching on like a never-ending detour. It wasn’t just about traffic anymore. It was about the people whose lives had been disrupted: the daily commuters stuck in gridlock, the businesses struggling to stay afloat, and the local Waffle House in Blacksburg that had to shut its doors and relocate, its new opening date still a mystery.  The thing is, I-85 doesn’t care. It doesn’t apologize for the mess or the inconvenience. It just keeps moving forward, one lane at a time, one detour after another, as long as the traffic can somehow keep flowing. It sounds arrogant, maybe even a little pompous, but I realized something: a lot of us are in the middle of our own construction projects. We’re in the messy, chaotic phase of rebuilding, of trying to get from where we are to where we want to be.  The problem is, most of us don’t treat it like a real construction site. We worry too much about what others think, about inconveniencing them, about whether they even care. But in the middle of our own I-85, we need to make definite goals, break them down into steps, and focus on the small wins that lead to the bigger picture. We need to block out the noise, the distractions, the people who don’t understand what we’re going through.  And we have to remember something important: just like I-85, we will never be fully complete. There will be milestones, yes, but also new detours, new challenges. The key is to keep the momentum going. Keep building, keep planning, keep moving forward. Because one thing I’ve never seen on a highway is a sign that says, “Construction complete.” And maybe that’s the point.  Our lives are always under construction. So keep building.

Use the evidence above when it is relevant. If it is not relevant, answer from the prompt.

I knew I was going to write a post the moment I saw the meme. Something about it struck a chord, like it was a sign or a nudge from the universe. The meme was about the ongoing construction on I-85 in South Carolina—years of it, stretching on like a never-ending detour. It wasn’t just about traffic anymore. It was about the people whose lives had been disrupted: the daily commuters stuck in gridlock, the businesses struggling to stay afloat, and the local Waffle House in Blacksburg that had to shut its doors and relocate, its new opening date still a mystery.

The thing is, I-85 doesn’t care. It doesn’t apologize for the mess or the inconvenience. It just keeps moving forward, one lane at a time, one detour after another, as long as the traffic can somehow keep flowing. It sounds arrogant, maybe even a little pompous, but I realized something: a lot of us are in the middle of our own construction projects. We’re in the messy, chaotic phase of rebuilding, of trying to get from where we are to where we want to be.

The problem is, most of us don’t treat it like a real construction site. We worry too much about what others think, about inconveniencing them, about whether they even care. But in the middle of our own I-85, we need to make definite goals, break them down into steps, and focus on the small wins that lead to the bigger picture. We need to block out the noise, the distractions, the people who don’t understand what we’re going through.

And we have to remember something important: just like I-85, we will never be fully complete. There will be milestones, yes, but also new detours, new challenges. The key is to keep the momentum going. Keep building, keep planning, keep moving forward. Because one thing I’ve never seen on a highway is a sign that says, “Construction complete.” And maybe that’s the point.

Our lives are always under construction. So keep building.

**Pregnancy, Workouts, and Staying Strong: Lucy Mecklenburgh Shares Her Journey**  When Lucy Mecklenburgh, CEO of *Results with Lucy* and one of the UK’s most followed fitness influencers, announced her pregnancy three months ago, her 1.5 million Instagram followers were as excited as she was. True to her word, Lucy has been open and honest about every step of her journey—and we recently caught up with her to hear more about how pregnancy is shaping her fitness, mindset, and body.  **First of all, congratulations on your fantastic news! How has pregnancy been for you so far?**  Lucy: “The first three months were a real challenge. I suffered with pregnancy sickness most days, and it felt like a three-month hangover from hell! I couldn’t work out and I was living on beige and bland food, which was really hard for someone like me. But I’m so grateful to say that in the second trimester, I feel so much better. The sickness has eased off, and I have so much more energy. I’m back to doing four to five 30-minute workouts a week!”  **How are you adjusting to your new body shape?**  Lucy: “It’s crazy, but I’ve never felt more confident and comfortable in my own skin—even though I’m getting bigger! I’m in awe of how incredible our bodies are when we’re pregnant. As someone who used to worry about bloating, it’s actually really nice to feel relaxed after eating, not worrying about how I look, and just breathing naturally. It’s a different kind of strength and beauty.”  **Have you had to change your workouts accordingly?**  Lucy: “Yes, my personal trainer and co-founder of *Results with Lucy*, Cecilia Harris, has adjusted my workouts. I used to always finish with 10 minutes of ab work, like crunches—but that’s out now. We focus more on pelvic floor exercises instead. I also haven’t been pushing myself to exhaustion. My workouts are now lower impact, and no more burpees (yay!). It’s all about listening to your body and understanding that you can still be active, even if you can’t do as much as before. Right now, I’m really enjoying long walks.”  **What would you say to someone who says you shouldn’t be exercising while pregnant?**  Lucy: “Don’t be scared of exercise, but do listen to your body. If, like me, you suffer from extreme sickness or just don’t have the energy, it’s okay to ease off. I didn’t work out in my first trimester, but I still went on a few walks, which helped a lot. If you do want to stay active, there are so many prenatal workouts, safety videos, and blogs on *Results with Lucy*. Educate yourself first so you know exactly what you can and can’t do—so you feel confident that it’s safe for you and your baby.”  **We wish you all the best with the rest of your pregnancy! What are the next steps to prepare for the little one?**  Lucy: “A lot of reading, for sure—it’s all new to me! I’m also really excited about getting the nursery ready. This is such a special time, and I’m just so grateful for the support from my team and my followers.”  **If, like Lucy, you love walking, send us your snaps and we’ll share them on our Instagram. Just use #WalkToWellbeing.**  Wishing Lucy all the best as she continues her journey into motherhood—and proving once again that strength, confidence, and fitness can look different at every stage of life.

**Pregnancy, Workouts, and Staying Strong: Lucy Mecklenburgh Shares Her Journey**

When Lucy Mecklenburgh, CEO of *Results with Lucy* and one of the UK’s most followed fitness influencers, announced her pregnancy three months ago, her 1.5 million Instagram followers were as excited as she was. True to her word, Lucy has been open and honest about every step of her journey—and we recently caught up with her to hear more about how pregnancy is shaping her fitness, mindset, and body.

**First of all, congratulations on your fantastic news! How has pregnancy been for you so far?**

Lucy: “The first three months were a real challenge. I suffered with pregnancy sickness most days, and it felt like a three-month hangover from hell! I couldn’t work out and I was living on beige and bland food, which was really hard for someone like me. But I’m so grateful to say that in the second trimester, I feel so much better. The sickness has eased off, and I have so much more energy. I’m back to doing four to five 30-minute workouts a week!”

**How are you adjusting to your new body shape?**

Lucy: “It’s crazy, but I’ve never felt more confident and comfortable in my own skin—even though I’m getting bigger! I’m in awe of how incredible our bodies are when we’re pregnant. As someone who used to worry about bloating, it’s actually really nice to feel relaxed after eating, not worrying about how I look, and just breathing naturally. It’s a different kind of strength and beauty.”

**Have you had to change your workouts accordingly?**

Lucy: “Yes, my personal trainer and co-founder of *Results with Lucy*, Cecilia Harris, has adjusted my workouts. I used to always finish with 10 minutes of ab work, like crunches—but that’s out now. We focus more on pelvic floor exercises instead. I also haven’t been pushing myself to exhaustion. My workouts are now lower impact, and no more burpees (yay!). It’s all about listening to your body and understanding that you can still be active, even if you can’t do as much as before. Right now, I’m really enjoying long walks.”

**What would you say to someone who says you shouldn’t be exercising while pregnant?**

Lucy: “Don’t be scared of exercise, but do listen to your body. If, like me, you suffer from extreme sickness or just don’t have the energy, it’s okay to ease off. I didn’t work out in my first trimester, but I still went on a few walks, which helped a lot. If you do want to stay active, there are so many prenatal workouts, safety videos, and blogs on *Results with Lucy*. Educate yourself first so you know exactly what you can and can’t do—so you feel confident that it’s safe for you and your baby.”

**We wish you all the best with the rest of your pregnancy! What are the next steps to prepare for the little one?**

Lucy: “A lot of reading, for sure—it’s all new to me! I’m also really excited about getting the nursery ready. This is such a special time, and I’m just so grateful for the support from my team and my followers.”

**If, like Lucy, you love walking, send us your snaps and we’ll share them on our Instagram. Just use #WalkToWellbeing.**

Wishing Lucy all the best as she continues her journey into motherhood—and proving once again that strength, confidence, and fitness can look different at every stage of life.

[Your Name]   [Your Address]   [City, State, ZIP Code]   [Email Address]   [Date]    To Whom It May Concern,    I am writing in response to your inquiry regarding Gary Paffett’s recent experience at the Vallelunga circuit and his insights on the new Mercedes-AMG C 63 DTM and the upcoming Hankook tyres.    Gary Paffett, in his first visit to Vallelunga, shared that he has had limited opportunities to learn new tracks in recent years. To prepare for this challenge, he utilized a computer simulation, a tool not available in his earlier racing days. Additionally, he worked closely with the engineering team upon arrival and analyzed data from Robert’s prior session. Gary emphasized the importance of pushing the car to its limits during testing, which required careful preparation to maximize the time available.    Regarding the homologation of the new Mercedes-AMG C 63 DTM, Gary explained that the car tested in Vallelunga is not the final version to be used at the season opener in Hockenheim. The team is currently in an interim phase, with the primary focus on understanding the performance of the tyres and mechanical components.    When asked about the new generation of Hankook tyres for 2017, Gary noted that the softer compound is a welcome change, as it enhances performance and driving feel. However, the increased degradation rate presents a challenge, requiring a more measured driving style to avoid excessive wear. Drivers must develop a strong sense of tyre condition without direct data, relying instead on feel and experience. Gary stressed the importance of test sessions in learning how the tyres behave over long runs, which will be crucial for success in the upcoming season.    Sincerely,   [Your Name]

Additionally, he worked closely with the engineering team upon arrival and analyzed data from Robert’s prior session. Gary emphasized the importance of pushing the car to its limits during testing, which required careful preparation to maximize the time available.  

Regarding the homologation of the new Mercedes-AMG C 63 DTM, Gary explained that the car tested in Vallelunga is not the final version to be used at the season opener in Hockenheim. The team is currently in an interim phase, with the primary focus on understanding the performance of the tyres and mechanical components.  

When asked about the new generation of Hankook tyres for 2017, Gary noted that the softer compound is a welcome change, as it enhances performance and driving feel. However, the increased degradation rate presents a challenge, requiring a more measured driving style to avoid excessive wear. Drivers must develop a strong sense of tyre condition without direct data, relying instead on feel and experience. Gary stressed the importance of test sessions in learning how the tyres behave over long runs, which will be crucial for success in the upcoming season.

I am writing in response to your inquiry regarding Gary Paffett’s recent experience at the Vallelunga circuit and his insights on the new Mercedes-AMG C 63 DTM and the upcoming Hankook tyres.  

Gary Paffett, in his first visit to Vallelunga, shared that he has had limited opportunities to learn new tracks in recent years. To prepare for this challenge, he utilized a computer simulation, a tool not available in his earlier racing days. Additionally, he worked closely with the engineering team upon arrival and analyzed data from Robert’s prior session. Gary emphasized the importance of pushing the car to its limits during testing, which required careful preparation to maximize the time available.  

Regarding the homologation of the new Mercedes-AMG C 63 DTM, Gary explained that the car tested in Vallelunga is not the final version to be used at the season opener in Hockenheim. The team is currently in an interim phase, with the primary focus on understanding the performance of the tyres and mechanical components.  

When asked about the new generation of Hankook tyres for 2017, Gary noted that the softer compound is a welcome change, as it enhances performance and driving feel. However, the increased degradation rate presents a challenge, requiring a more measured driving style to avoid excessive wear. Drivers must develop a strong sense of tyre condition without direct data, relying instead on feel and experience. Gary stressed the importance of test sessions in learning how the tyres behave over long runs, which will be crucial for success in the upcoming season.  

Sincerely,  
[Your Name]

**Interviewer:** Can you tell us how Kamal and Jay's love story began?  **Expert:** Absolutely! Their love story began at a welcome party at TRU University in Canada. What started as a simple meet-up turned into something much more. Their friendship grew so deep that it eventually blossomed into love.  **Interviewer:** What qualities in Jay stood out to Kamal?  **Expert:** Kamal often speaks highly of Jay. She describes him as a very strong and intelligent man who truly cares about her. In fact, she says, “He really, really, really cares about me. He's a good man. A very good man. He'll keep me happy, I know that.”  **Interviewer:** The wedding sounds absolutely magical. Can you walk us through the Punjabi wedding celebration?  **Expert:** Oh, it was a dream come true! The celebration began with a *sarson ki khet wali mehndi*—a traditional Punjabi pre-wedding ritual. The bride looked stunning in a simple green suit as she sat in a mustard field, getting her hands adorned with beautiful bridal *mehndi* designs. It was all things Bollywood, reminiscent of the *DDLJ* days, where love and romance take center stage.  **Interviewer:** What about the sangeet? Was it anything out of the ordinary?  **Expert:** The sangeet was absolutely unique! It featured never-seen-before *parande* (canopy) decor that was incredibly creative and visually stunning. The entire event was a blend of tradition and innovation.  **Interviewer:** How was the actual wedding ceremony?  **Expert:** The intimate wedding took place at a gurudwara, in the presence of close family and friends. Kamal added a western touch by entering with a wedding bouquet in hand, which was a lovely contrast to the traditional Punjabi setting.  **Interviewer:** Who was responsible for capturing the memories of this beautiful day?  **Expert:** The photos and videos were beautifully captured by Ananya Rijhwani and her team of brilliant photographers. They managed to capture every candid moment, every warm hug, and every heartfelt emotion perfectly. It was truly a celebration to remember!

Today’s #VeteranOfTheDay is Army Veteran Sharon Vargas. Sharon served in the U.S. Army from 1972 to 1992, participating in several military operations, including those in Vietnam and during the Gulf War.  Originally from Ambler, Pennsylvania, Sharon joined the Army during the Women’s Army Corps (WAC) expansion in Philadelphia. She became one of the first female recruiters in the United States following the integration of WAC into the regular Army. Sharon spent 18 years with the Army Recruiting Command, relocating frequently as part of her assignments.  Throughout her military career, Sharon was recognized with numerous awards, including the Meritorious Service Medal and multiple Army Achievement Medals.  After retiring from the Army, Sharon returned to school and earned her Bachelor’s degree through the Department of Veterans Affairs. She has since worked in Human Resources and most recently served as a job search coach. Sharon values her veteran status and the formative years she spent in the Army. “It’s because I joined the Army that I am who I am today,” she said.  Sharon currently resides just outside of Philadelphia.  Thank you for your service, Sharon.  Are you looking for a meaningful way to honor a special Veteran in your life? The Department of Veterans Affairs’ #VeteranOfTheDay social media feature offers a unique opportunity to recognize and celebrate their service.  Nominating a Veteran is simple—just send an email to firstname.lastname@example.com with detailed information and quality photographs. For tips on creating a strong submission, visit the VA’s blog post on the nomination process.  Graphic by Kierra Willis: Kierra Willis is a Graphic Communication major at the University of Maryland University College and holds an Associate of Applied Science degree in Graphic Design and Visual Communications.

If you’ve ever found yourself craving a taste of the South while wandering the streets of Los Angeles, you’re not alone. Southern food is a rare gem in this sprawling city, and finding a *really* good version of it feels like winning the lottery. But now, thanks to the culinary vision of Govind Armstrong and Brad Johnson, that craving can finally be satisfied.  Enter **Post and Beam** — a restaurant that brings the soul of Southern cuisine to the heart of LA, with a modern twist that makes it feel fresh and exciting. This isn’t just any comfort food; it’s a love letter to the flavors of the South, reimagined for the modern Angeleno. From classic shrimp and grits to hand-stretched pizzas, the menu is a delightful journey through the best of Southern cooking.  What makes Post and Beam stand out is its ability to deliver big, bold flavors without breaking the bank. It’s the kind of place where you can enjoy a generous plate of food that hits all the right notes — hearty, comforting, and just a little bit indulgent. Whether you're looking for a casual bite or a date night spot, this restaurant checks all the boxes.  And if you’re dining through DineLA, you’ll get even more value for your dollar. For the amount of food we received and the price we paid, it was truly a **bang for your buck** — and your taste buds.  So next time your heart is longing for a taste of the South, skip the flight and head straight to Post and Beam. Your soul — and your stomach — will thank you.

If you’ve ever found yourself craving a taste of the South while wandering the streets of Los Angeles, you’re not alone. Southern food is a rare gem in this sprawling city, and finding a *really* good version of it feels like winning the lottery. But now, thanks to the culinary vision of Govind Armstrong and Brad Johnson, that craving can finally be satisfied.

Enter **Post and Beam** — a restaurant that brings the soul of Southern cuisine to the heart of LA, with a modern twist that makes it feel fresh and exciting. This isn’t just any comfort food; it’s a love letter to the flavors of the South, reimagined for the modern Angeleno. From classic shrimp and grits to hand-stretched pizzas, the menu is a delightful journey through the best of Southern cooking.

What makes Post and Beam stand out is its ability to deliver big, bold flavors without breaking the bank. It’s the kind of place where you can enjoy a generous plate of food that hits all the right notes — hearty, comforting, and just a little bit indulgent. Whether you're looking for a casual bite or a date night spot, this restaurant checks all the boxes.

And if you’re dining through DineLA, you’ll get even more value for your dollar. For the amount of food we received and the price we paid, it was truly a **bang for your buck** — and your taste buds.

So next time your heart is longing for a taste of the South, skip the flight and head straight to Post and Beam. Your soul — and your stomach — will thank you.

evidence_spans:
**Interviewer:** Good afternoon. Today we’re speaking with the Spokesman of the Defense Ministry of Azerbaijan. Thank you for joining us. What is the significance of the 15th anniversary of Azerbaijan’s cooperation with NATO?

**Spokesman:** This anniversary is a significant milestone that highlights the progress made in the military development of Azerbaijan. In 1994, Azerbaijan joined NATO's Partnership for Peace program, and since then, our armed forces have achieved notable advancements through this cooperation.

**Interviewer:** How has this cooperation with NATO impacted the National Army of Azerbaijan?

**Spokesman:** Our cooperation with NATO has greatly enhanced the capabilities of the National Army. Through various programs and joint activities, we have improved our military standards, training, and operational readiness. This has also enabled us to contribute more effectively to international security.

**Interviewer:** In what ways has Azerbaijan contributed to international peace and security?

**Spokesman:** Azerbaijan has actively participated in peacekeeping missions in regions such as Kosovo and Iraq. Currently, our peacekeeping contingent in Afghanistan has doubled and now numbers 90 personnel. This demonstrates our commitment to global peace and stability.

**Interviewer:** How many Azerbaijani military personnel have been involved in NATO activities in the past year?

**Spokesman:** Thousands of Azerbaijani military personnel have taken part in more than 200 NATO-related activities over the past year. These activities include training, joint exercises, and peacekeeping missions.

**Interviewer:** Are there other forms of military cooperation beyond NATO?

**Spokesman:** Yes, Azerbaijan is also engaged in military cooperation with various countries through bilateral agreements. We currently have agreements with nearly 30 countries, and we are working on establishing similar agreements with another 20 nations.

**Interviewer:** What does the interest of leading countries in cooperating with Azerbaijan indicate?

**Spokesman:** The willingness of major global powers, including countries in America, Europe, and the Middle East, to cooperate with Azerbaijan in the military field is a clear indication of the strength and professionalism of our armed forces today.

[Your Name]   [Your Address]   [City, State, ZIP Code]   [Email Address]   [Date]    To Whom It May Concern,    I am writing to address a matter I have previously raised regarding the financial and emotional burdens borne by Ultra Orthodox Jewish women who support their husbands while the latter devote the majority of their time to religious studies. Recently, women living this reality—many of whom are experiencing severe burnout—have shared insights into their lives, and I believe it is important to bring attention to their experiences.    The following excerpts, translated from an article (a link to which is provided for those who read Hebrew), capture the essence of their voices:    > “We are collapsing. We work three jobs: maintaining the home, educating the children, and a full-time shift outside the home. We all know what a scholar husband contributes financially (a meager scholarship – my note). And it has only gotten worse with the economic crisis. We, like most of our community, have large families. We do not hire household help. With great pain in our hearts, we are forced to ask our older daughters to step up to the task, and these girls, even before they are married, already carry a burden which will be even heavier once they are wives.”    > “Was it the way of Torah in the past too – that the entire pressure of maintaining and financing the household would fall on the woman's shoulders? Isn't it a change of the last generation or two? Once, nearly all the wives of religious scholars held teaching jobs. We had plenty of vacation time. Our work day was finished early and we could return home before our children. Today most of us work in fields that require great emotional resources and many hours of exhausting effort. And only then we come back and begin the full-time second shift – which is actually the first for us.”    I am not one to deny the importance of having full-time Torah scholars, especially in our generation of spiritual and moral decline. However, when financial resources are scarce, I find it surreal that a man may immerse himself in the spiritual while turning a blind eye to the very real plight of his wife and children.    The ketubah, the Jewish marriage contract, states clearly that the husband is responsible for supporting his wife, not the other way around. While there is a provision allowing a wife to support her husband if she is willing, there is immense pressure on young women in the charedi community to choose scholar husbands, a status considered more noble and prestigious. I am not convinced that all these young women fully understand the implications of such a choice, or are truly prepared to become primary wage-earners while raising large families and maintaining immaculate homes.    Here is a question that some may consider sacrilegious: Are all those who study Torah full-time so brilliant that our generation would suffer a terrible loss if they worked at least part of the time? Certainly, we need scholars, but I do not believe that every boy should be encouraged to become one.    Perhaps, if there were fewer full-time scholars, and if scholarships were awarded only to the most talented, there could be more resources for each recipient. In such a scenario, some scholars could work part-time, and the burden on women could be significantly reduced.    I hope this letter brings attention to an issue that deserves serious consideration and discussion.    Sincerely,   [Your Name]

Use the evidence above when it is relevant. If it is not relevant, answer from the prompt.

Claim: Ultra Orthodox Jewish husbands engaged in full-time Torah study are not fulfilling their ketubah obligation to financially support their wives, as the women bear primary responsibility for household finances.

To Whom It May Concern,  

I am writing to address a matter I have previously raised regarding the financial and emotional burdens borne by Ultra Orthodox Jewish women who support their husbands while the latter devote the majority of their time to religious studies. Recently, women living this reality—many of whom are experiencing severe burnout—have shared insights into their lives, and I believe it is important to bring attention to their experiences.  

The following excerpts, translated from an article (a link to which is provided for those who read Hebrew), capture the essence of their voices:  

> “We are collapsing. We work three jobs: maintaining the home, educating the children, and a full-time shift outside the home. We all know what a scholar husband contributes financially (a meager scholarship – my note). And it has only gotten worse with the economic crisis. We, like most of our community, have large families. We do not hire household help. With great pain in our hearts, we are forced to ask our older daughters to step up to the task, and these girls, even before they are married, already carry a burden which will be even heavier once they are wives.”  

> “Was it the way of Torah in the past too – that the entire pressure of maintaining and financing the household would fall on the woman's shoulders? Isn't it a change of the last generation or two? Once, nearly all the wives of religious scholars held teaching jobs. We had plenty of vacation time. Our work day was finished early and we could return home before our children. Today most of us work in fields that require great emotional resources and many hours of exhausting effort. And only then we come back and begin the full-time second shift – which is actually the first for us.”  

I am not one to deny the importance of having full-time Torah scholars, especially in our generation of spiritual and moral decline. However, when financial resources are scarce, I find it surreal that a man may immerse himself in the spiritual while turning a blind eye to the very real plight of his wife and children.  

The ketubah, the Jewish marriage contract, states clearly that the husband is responsible for supporting his wife, not the other way around. While there is a provision allowing a wife to support her husband if she is willing, there is immense pressure on young women in the charedi community to choose scholar husbands, a status considered more noble and prestigious. I am not convinced that all these young women fully understand the implications of such a choice, or are truly prepared to become primary wage-earners while raising large families and maintaining immaculate homes.  

Here is a question that some may consider sacrilegious: Are all those who study Torah full-time so brilliant that our generation would suffer a terrible loss if they worked at least part of the time? Certainly, we need scholars, but I do not believe that every boy should be encouraged to become one.  

Perhaps, if there were fewer full-time scholars, and if scholarships were awarded only to the most talented, there could be more resources for each recipient. In such a scenario, some scholars could work part-time, and the burden on women could be significantly reduced.  

I hope this letter brings attention to an issue that deserves serious consideration and discussion.  

Sincerely,  
[Your Name]

**Formal Report: The Role of Struggle and Relationships in Achieving Happiness**  **Introduction**   The pursuit of happiness is a universal human aspiration. While many people associate happiness with external achievements such as financial security, romantic relationships, or material success, these are not the fundamental sources of lasting happiness. This report explores the idea that struggle and strong, supportive relationships are essential components in the development of true happiness.  **Struggle as a Source of Happiness**   Contrary to popular belief, happiness is not derived from ease or comfort. Rather, it is born from effort and perseverance. When individuals work hard toward a goal—whether it is mastering a new skill, completing a challenging project, or achieving a physical milestone—the satisfaction that follows is deeply fulfilling. This emotional payoff is directly proportional to the difficulty of the task. In contrast, success that comes without effort often lacks emotional impact and is quickly forgotten.  Personal experience supports this notion. Starting a new business, for example, is consistently more difficult than anticipated. Despite prior experience, each venture presents new and unexpected challenges. The process is demanding, often requiring long hours, problem-solving, and resilience. However, the emotional reward at the end is immense. It is through this struggle that a sense of accomplishment and fulfillment is achieved. Without the effort, the joy of success is diminished.  **The Psychological Impact of Adversity**   Adversity plays a crucial role in shaping a person's character and emotional resilience. Facing and overcoming hardship provides perspective, making moments of success more meaningful. The contrast between struggle and achievement enhances the emotional highs, making them more profound and memorable. This dynamic is essential to the experience of happiness.  **The Importance of Strong Relationships**   In addition to struggle, strong, steady relationships form a critical foundation for happiness. A supportive network of family, friends, and colleagues provides emotional security and strength during difficult times. Maintaining these relationships requires effort and intentionality. By surrounding oneself with positive, influential people, individuals can create a sense of community and belonging that sustains them through challenges.  This network is often referred to as "the family"—a group of individuals who support, challenge, and uplift one another. The principles of loyalty, mutual support, and shared success are central to this dynamic. These relationships act as a stabilizing force, helping individuals stay grounded and motivated in their pursuits.  **Conclusion**   Happiness is not the result of easy success or material gain. Rather, it is cultivated through struggle and supported by meaningful relationships. The effort required to achieve goals, combined with a strong support system, creates the conditions for lasting happiness. By embracing challenges and nurturing positive connections, individuals can build a life filled with fulfillment and joy. Therefore, it is essential to recognize that the path to happiness is paved with effort, resilience, and love.

Use the evidence above when it is relevant. If it is not relevant, answer from the prompt.

[Your Name]   [Your Address]   [City, State, ZIP Code]   [Email Address]   [Date]    To Whom It May Concern,    I hope this letter finds you well. I am writing to share my experience with a promising paper I have recently discovered for Pt/Pd printing. The paper is exceptionally smooth and capable of withstanding an extended soak time—up to several hours—without showing any signs of dog ears or surface fibre separation. I have affectionately named it “Sahara Satin,” due to its thirsty nature and the way the coating migrates across its surface, much like a nomadic tribe.    The issue I am encountering is as follows: the paper absorbs the first coat deeply and evenly, resulting in a print with a beautifully smooth tone, albeit slightly anaemic. When a second coat is applied, a peculiar migration pattern begins. It starts from the centre of the previous coat and moves outward toward the edge, then reverses direction and returns toward the centre. This results in a print with a rich, black velvet border, but the tonal continuity diminishes toward the image’s centre.    A third coat reduces the migration, but introduces a distinct veiling effect, most noticeable in the black tones. I am aware that various studies have indicated that gelatine can inhibit platinum chemistry, so I suspect this may not be a viable solution in my case.    I would greatly appreciate any suggestions or methods you may have for working with this paper—overcoats, undercoats, or even unconventional approaches such as “sweater vests.” Anything that might help me achieve the desired results would be most welcome.    Should I discover a successful method for using this beautiful paper, I will be sure to share the details here for the benefit of others.    Thank you for your time and consideration.    Yours sincerely,   [Your Name]

Use the evidence above when it is relevant. If it is not relevant, answer from the prompt.

WASHINGTON — A new survey by the Washington Post and Pew Research Center reveals that Americans have largely negative opinions of the intense budget debate that has dominated Washington in recent weeks, with many now viewing the nation's political leaders less favorably.   When asked to describe the budget negotiations in one word, the most common responses—gathered just before a deal seemed to be reached—were “ridiculous,” “disgusting,” and “stupid.” About 73 percent of Americans used a negative term, while only 2 percent offered a positive one. “Ridiculous” was the most commonly used word across party lines and had also been the top response in a previous poll about the narrowly avoided government shutdown. In the latest survey, the top 27 words were negative, with the top 10 including “frustrating,” “poor,” “terrible,” “disappointing,” “childish,” “messy,” and “joke.”  When asked to reflect on their views over the past few weeks, 37 percent of respondents said they now view President Obama less favorably, more than double the 18 percent who said their view of him had improved. About 44 percent said their opinions remained unchanged, but the overall negative shift could further damage the president’s already low approval ratings in Post-ABC News and Pew polls.  For House Speaker John Boehner, the numbers are even more unfavorable, with three times as many people saying their opinion of him has worsened compared to those who said it improved (34 percent less favorable; 11 percent more favorable). However, Boehner seems to have gained support among tea party Republicans, with 54 percent of them saying they now view him more positively. Notably, this shift didn’t come at the expense of other Republicans, as 61 percent of non-tea party Republicans said their opinions of the speaker had not changed.  On the Democratic side, while liberal Democrats have been the fiercest critics of President Obama from within their own party, the new poll shows that moderate and conservative Democrats are three times more likely than liberal Democrats to say their view of Obama has worsened in recent weeks. Only 7 percent of liberal Democrats said their opinion had declined, with 55 percent reporting no change and 37 percent saying it had improved.  The budget debate also appears to have done little to improve perceptions among political independents. Just 13 percent now view Obama more favorably (38 percent less so), and only 7 percent now see Boehner in a more positive light. The negotiations also failed to improve views of Congress, with nearly 20 percent of independents saying they now view both Democrats and Republicans in Congress more negatively, and not a single independent said they now have a more favorable view of both parties.  The Washington Post-ABC News poll was conducted by phone from July 14 to 17 among a random national sample of 1,001 adults, including users of both landlines and cell phones. The full survey has a margin of sampling error of plus or minus 3.5 percentage points. Sampling, data collection, and tabulation were conducted by Abt-SRBI of New York.

Use the evidence above when it is relevant. If it is not relevant, answer from the prompt.

[Your Name]   [Your Designation]   [Your Organization]   [Address]   [City, State, Pin Code]   [Date]    To,   The Union Ministry of Defence   [Address]   [City, State, Pin Code]    Subject: Proposal for Inclusion of Hindustan Shipyard Limited in P-75 (I) Project    Sir/Madam,    I would like to bring to your kind attention the opportunity that the decision of the Union Ministry of Defence to augment the submarine fleet and place an order for the indigenous construction of six submarines under the 'Make in India' campaign presents for Hindustan Shipyard Limited (HSL).    HSL, which came under the Ministry of Defence in February 2010, possesses one of the most advanced ecosystems in the world for submarine building. The yard has a proven track record in EKM submarine repair and has successfully contributed to the construction of the nuclear-powered Arihant submarine at the Ship Building Centre. Additionally, HSL has a pool of trained contractors with extensive experience in submarine repairs and construction. The yard has already invested Rs. 50 crore in setting up state-of-the-art submarine repair facilities.    The proposed order for six submarines, each valued at Rs. 10,000 crore, could significantly strengthen HSL's financial position. HSL Chairman and Managing Director, Rear Admiral N.K. Mishra, has expressed confidence in the yard's ability to construct all six submarines under the P-75 (I) Project and deliver them on time, citing the yard's proven expertise in the more complex domain of submarine repairs.    To further bolster its claim for the submarine construction order, HSL has formed a consortium with Bharat Heavy Electricals Limited (BHEL) and M/s MIDHANI. This collaboration is expected to synergise BHEL’s manufacturing capabilities, MIDHANI’s material technology, and HSL’s shipbuilding expertise.    Established in 1941, HSL was the first Greenfield shipyard in India, set up by industrialist Walchand Hirachand under the Scindia Steam Navigation Company. The shipyard was strategically located in Visakhapatnam due to its strategic significance, as envisioned during the World War period. After being under the Ministry of Shipping and facing a critical financial situation due to a lack of orders, HSL was taken over by the Ministry of Defence four years ago with the assurance of being considered for Navy orders on a nomination basis.    It is heartening to note that, through the sustained efforts of the workforce and management, HSL's net losses have been on a declining trend—from Rs. 86 crore in 2011-12 to Rs. 55 crore in 2012-13 and Rs. 46 crore in 2013-14.    In view of its capabilities, strategic location, and commitment to excellence, I respectfully request that HSL be considered for the submarine construction project under the P-75 (I) initiative.    Thanking you,    Yours faithfully,   [Your Name]   [Your Designation]   [Your Contact Information]

Use the evidence above when it is relevant. If it is not relevant, answer from the prompt.

GoBear is a one-stop online service that helps you compare a wide range of financial products, including travel insurance, credit cards, and soon to be launched, home loans, personal loans, and car insurance. Designed as a metasearch engine specifically for financial services, GoBear uses a sophisticated algorithm to rank and present the most relevant options, giving you all the essential information you need to make an informed decision.  As the first of its kind in Asia, GoBear is revolutionizing how consumers shop for financial products. Whether you're looking for the best travel insurance or the most suitable credit card, GoBear simplifies the process by showcasing over 40 travel insurance options and 192 credit cards, with more categories to follow.  GoBear generates revenue through a transparent and unbiased model: product providers pay for each qualified click from GoBear to their site. These clicks are high-quality, as users must complete a few steps and provide details before being directed to a partner’s page, ensuring a high conversion rate for businesses.  With over 1.2 million users and 50% month-on-month growth since its launch in Singapore and Thailand, GoBear is quickly becoming the go-to platform for smart financial decisions.

John Oram, a retired design engineer from Torquay, Devon, had lived a long and steady life—until the day a sneeze changed everything. At 79, he had been managing a heart condition and diabetes, carefully taking Warfarin, a blood-thinning medication. His family and care home staff thought little of the routine moments that marked his daily life—until that fateful day in July.  On July 16, John was seen sneezing violently by two members of staff at the care home where he resided. The sneeze was so forceful it caught their attention. What seemed like a simple, harmless reaction soon turned tragic. Within hours, John collapsed. He was rushed to the hospital but passed away two days later, on July 18.  At the inquest into his death, the cause was revealed to be a brain haemorrhage and heart attack, both triggered by the violent sneeze. Dr. John Bridger, who conducted the post-mortem, found no signs of underlying trauma or skull fractures, but noted a subdural haematoma and coronary artery disease. The coroner, Ian Arrow, concluded the death was accidental.  Warfarin had made John's body more vulnerable to bleeding, and the sneeze, though seemingly minor, likely caused enough internal trauma to prove fatal. “It was the first case of 'death by sneezing'” I’ve seen in 24 years,” said Ric Parsons, a South Devon Coroner officer, describing the event as a “very sad end to his life.”  John’s son, Mark, was heartbroken. “He was sorely missed,” he said. “It just seems so unusual that something as innocuous as a sneeze could have killed him.” He acknowledged his father’s medical history—30 years of heart issues—had made him more fragile with age. “I guess as you get older, everything gets weaker, more susceptible to being damaged.”  For a man who had spent his life designing and building things, it was a cruel twist that his life ended in such an unexpected and fragile way.

Use the evidence above when it is relevant. If it is not relevant, answer from the prompt.

I recently visited the Arkansas State Veterans Cemetery to witness the funeral of Private William Andrew Long, and I must say it was a deeply moving and solemn experience. The event was held with full military honors, a fitting tribute to a young man who had just completed basic training and was preparing to serve his country in Korea. The atmosphere was respectful and heartfelt, with members of the Patriot Guard Riders and representatives from all military branches in attendance to show their support and respect.  Private Long was remembered not only for his service but for the kind, loving person he was. His family and friends spoke of the void his passing has left, but they focused on celebrating his life and the values he stood for. Governor Mike Beebe was also present, and his words reflected the deep sadness and confusion over the senseless violence that took a young life right here at home.  The ceremony was a powerful reminder of the sacrifices made by our service members and the importance of supporting them. Private Long was posthumously awarded the Army Commendation Medal, a recognition of his dedication and bravery. His story is a tragic one, but the way his community came together to honor him was a beautiful display of unity and patriotism. I left the event with a renewed appreciation for the men and women who serve our country and the families who support them.

**Interviewer:** Thank you for joining us today. To start, can you tell us about the research you conducted on Joseph H. Shively and what you discovered?  **Expert:** Absolutely. Genealogical records for the children of John B. Shively and his wife Sarah Heaverin have been well-documented, with the exception of their son Joseph H. Shively. Through my research, I was able to uncover a number of records that had previously been missing or overlooked. Joseph’s life was complicated by the fact that he lived in several different locations, which made tracking his history more challenging.  **Interviewer:** What can you tell us about Joseph H. Shively’s early life and death?  **Expert:** Joseph H. Shively was born on November 17, 1844, in Taylor County, Kentucky. He passed away on September 29, 1917, at the National Home for Disabled Volunteer Soldiers in Dayton, Montgomery County, Ohio. He was buried in Calvary Cemetery in Terre Haute, Vigo County, Indiana. The tombstone image you might have seen was provided by a volunteer on Find A Grave.  **Interviewer:** I understand he was admitted to a veterans' home. Is it possible to see Laylat al-Qadr with the human eye? Some believe that if someone can see it, they might notice a light in the sky or similar signs. How did the Messenger of Allaah (peace and blessings of Allaah be upon him) and the Sahaabah (may Allaah be pleased with them) perceive it? How can a person be certain that they have encountered Laylat al-Qadr? And if someone does not see that night, will they still receive its reward? Please clarify this for us and provide the supporting evidence.  There is a reliable narration from the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allaah be upon him) indicating that one of the signs of Laylat al-Qadr is that the sun rises the next morning without any visible rays. Ubayy ibn Ka’b used to assert that it occurred on the twenty-seventh night, basing this on that sign. However, the more accurate opinion is that Laylat al-Qadr shifts among the last ten nights of Ramadan, though it is most likely to occur on one of the odd-numbered nights, with the twenty-seventh being the most probable. Whoever dedicates themselves to worship during all of these nights—through prayer, recitation of the Qur’aan, making du’aa’, and other righteous deeds—will surely encounter Laylat al-Qadr and attain the blessings that Allaah has promised to those who act with faith and in pursuit of reward.  And Allaah is the Provider of strength. May Allaah bless our Prophet Muhammad and his family and companions.

Use the evidence above when it is relevant. If it is not relevant, answer from the prompt.

So what does this mean to me? Land that drinks in the rain speaks to me of constant growth—of a place where life can thrive and bear fruit. But there's a warning here too: if you're in a place of fruitfulness, you should be bearing fruit. When the Word takes root in your heart and you're surrounded by strong, godly people, shouldn't you be growing? The answer is yes. If you're not—why not? I know I’ve been guilty of going through the motions, surrounded by spiritual richness, yet producing nothing but thorns and thistles. To bear fruit for God takes diligence, a heart that honors Him, and a life that doesn’t bring Him disgrace. These verses are a powerful reminder of what it means to live the Christian life. Lord, help me take these words to heart and heed the warning. 🌱🙏 #ChristianLiving #SpiritualGrowth #BearingFruit #GodsWord

Amid the general sense of celebration that surrounds this four-year occasion, I hope you're having a joyful leap day!   Knitting has been a bit slow (though I’m editing the 25 pages, which I consider a positive step). I’ve finished blocking my Woodland Shawl and am wearing it now—it brings me peace. And yesterday, while the boys had their haircuts, I started the ribbing on my first Jaywalker sock (knitting toe-up makes it seem more advanced than it actually is).    I did some thinking yesterday, and I believe I might actually be able to get a spinning wheel sometime early summer or late spring. I want a Kromski—I’ve heard great things about them as spinning wheels, and in my opinion, they are the most visually appealing wheels available. I’m set on having one that is both beautiful and functional, as that matters a lot to me. I’m leaning toward the Symphony model, but I also love the Polonaise, and I appreciate the castle wheels. I like the look and size of the Minstrel, and the Mazurka comes with a tensioned Lazy Kate, which is a big plus. I’m waiting on some details from one of the potential suppliers, but I hope to have everything figured out within a month or two. If anyone has experience with any of these models, I’d love to hear your suggestions!

I just looked at your website, and I'm absolutely blown away by your work—FANTASTIC!!!!!! If you're into black and white film photography, you should definitely consider running some simple tests to determine your film speed and development times for different subject brightness ranges. Experts like Fred Picker, Ansel Adams, Steve Simmons, and Phil Davis have all outlined effective methods for this. The basic idea is to expose enough to retain detail in the darker areas of your scene, and then develop to achieve the desired contrast in the more important mid to bright tones. The system can be as simple or complex as you want. For instance, I only use N, N-1.5, and N+1.5 development times, and I rely on variable contrast paper and dodging and burning for fine-tuning. Others take it to a much higher level of precision with custom exposure and development systems.

**Interviewer:** Thank you for joining us today, Mr. Boone. You’ve been a lifelong resident of Lake Oswego and have voiced some strong concerns about recent city developments. Can you explain what’s at the heart of your frustration?  **Stephen T. Boone:** Absolutely. I’m deeply concerned about the direction the city is taking. The majority of the people I’ve spoken to are tired of the development and spending over the past decade. It seems like we’ve been making decisions that don’t reflect the will of the people, but rather the interests of a few.  **Interviewer:** You’ve mentioned several projects—like the West End Building, the high school remodels, and athletic fields. How do you feel these projects have impacted the community?  **Stephen T. Boone:** Many of these projects have been ill-advised and have put the burden on taxpayers. The high school remodels were funded by the school district, not the city, yet we’re still left with the consequences. We had declining demographics—so why build for more students than we had? And now we have athletic fields that we have to rent out to other communities just to afford them. That’s not smart planning.  **Interviewer:** You also bring up the issue of livability. How do you think the city is affecting the quality of life for residents?  **Stephen T. Boone:** We have the scenic beauty of Lake Oswego and the short commute to Portland—those are two of the biggest draws. But instead of preserving that, we’re cramming in more people and cars. That leads to longer commutes, more traffic, and higher taxes. Where’s the livability in that?  **Interviewer:** There’s also the recent proposal to allow seven-story buildings in the Foothills area. What are your thoughts on that?  **Stephen T. Boone:** I think it’s absolutely crazy. We’re going to block the view of Mount Hood for everyone. That mountain is one of the most magnificent sights in the state, and it’s a big part of what makes Oswego special. We already planted trees in the center meridian on A Avenue to block the view—now we’re going to do it on a much larger scale for the benefit of a few developers.  **Interviewer:** You mentioned that some of the people making these decisions may move on after a few years. What’s your concern about that?  **Stephen T. Boone:** Exactly. These decisions are made in the name of “progress,” but the people who push for them often move on. The rest of us are left to live with the consequences. We’re stuck with the traffic, the taxes, and the diminishing beauty of our city.  **Interviewer:** What would you say is a better measure of progress for Lake Oswego?  **Stephen T. Boone:** Progress should be measured by clean air, blue skies, light traffic, lower taxes, and the ability for everyone to enjoy expansive views of Mount Hood. Lake Oswego has always been the jewel of the Portland area. I believe it still can be—if we choose to preserve what makes it special.  **Interviewer:** Thank you, Mr. Boone, for sharing your perspective.

Use the evidence above when it is relevant. If it is not relevant, answer from the prompt.

But instead of preserving that, we’re cramming in more people and cars. That leads to longer commutes, more traffic, and higher taxes. Where’s the livability in that?

**Interviewer:** There’s also the recent proposal to allow seven-story buildings in the Foothills area. What are your thoughts on that?

**Stephen T. Boone:** I think it’s absolutely crazy. We’re going to block the view of Mount Hood for everyone. That mountain is one of the most magnificent sights in the state, and it’s a big part of what makes Oswego special. We already planted trees in the center meridian on A Avenue to block the view—now we’re going to do it on a much larger scale for the benefit of a few developers.

**Interviewer:** You mentioned that some of the people making these decisions may move on after a few years. What’s your concern about that?

**Stephen T. Boone:** Exactly. These decisions are made in the name of “progress,” but the people who push for them often move on. The rest of us are left to live with the consequences. We’re stuck with the traffic, the taxes, and the diminishing beauty of our city.

**Interviewer:** What would you say is a better measure of progress for Lake Oswego?

**Stephen T. Boone:** Progress should be measured by clean air, blue skies, light traffic, lower taxes, and the ability for everyone to enjoy expansive views of Mount Hood. Lake Oswego has always been the jewel of the Portland area. I believe it still can be—if we choose to preserve what makes it special.

**Interviewer:** Thank you, Mr. Boone, for sharing your perspective.

I wait until I’m *out* of shampoo and then just borrow Sarah’s. Or I grab one of the last garbage bags and don’t even add it to the list.  **Sarah:** Ugh! Chris, you’re the worst. The next morning, I went to drive somewhere and not only was the gas light on, but the computer couldn’t even calculate how many miles to empty! It just had a star! The line was below E, and I can’t believe I didn’t run out of gas. I was ticked!  **Mike:** Wow, that’s rough. I completely get that some people love this type of art, but it’s just not my thing. My little sister wanted to check it out, and I’m all for new experiences, so I said yes! The only downside? On a New Orleans Hornets roster filled with talent—ten NBA first-round picks, five of them lottery selections—Brian Roberts stood out for all the right reasons. The 6-foot-1, 180-pound point guard wasn’t a name that came up in the 2008 draft. In fact, he went undrafted out of the University of Dayton, a reality that would have sent many young players packing. But not Roberts.  For four long years, he chased his dream in places far from the bright lights of the NBA. He played in Israel. He played in Germany. He worked. He waited. And when he finally got a chance to step onto an NBA preseason court, he was 26—unusual for a rookie, but not for a fighter like Roberts. On October 7, against the Orlando Magic, he made his debut.  And what a debut it was. Roberts didn’t just show up—he shined. Leading New Orleans to an 85-80 comeback victory, he dropped a team-high 17 points, proving he belonged. It wasn’t a fluke, either. Just weeks earlier, in the Las Vegas Summer League, Roberts had dazzled for the Hornets, scoring double-digit points in all five games. That performance caught the attention of the team, and on August 16, they signed him to a free-agent contract.  For Roberts, it was the end of a long and winding journey that had taken him across three continents. For the small Western Ohio community of Dayton, it was a moment of pride. Only one player from the University of Dayton had ever made it to an NBA regular season game since 1999—Chris Wright, who played for Golden State in 2011–12. Now, with Roberts signed to the Hornets, the city had something to cheer about once more. A hometown underdog had finally arrived in the big leagues.

Use the evidence above when it is relevant. If it is not relevant, answer from the prompt.
